standard_description:
Represent a fraction 1/b on a number line diagram by defining the interval from 0 to 1 as the whole and partitioning it into b equal parts. Recognize that each part has size 1/b and that the endpoint of the part based at 0 locates the number 1/b on the number line.
Grade 3 expectations in this domain are limited to fractions with denominators 2, 3, 4, 6, and 8.
subject: Math

problems.text:
Problem 3
Pre-unit
Label each tick mark with the number it represents. Explain your reasoning.

khan_exercises.title: Recognize fractions greater than 1
khan_exercises.description: Identify the fraction of a whole that is shaded.  Fractions are greater than or equal to one.

items.question_content:
Each circle represents $1$ whole.



[[☃ image 1]]

**What fraction does the total area shaded represent?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
The $\purpleD{\text{numerator}}$ is the number of equal parts being counted.

The $\blueD{\text{denominator}}$ is the number of equal parts in **one** whole. 

$\dfrac{\purpleD{\text{numerator}}}{\blueD{\text{denominator}}}$

Each whole is divided into $\blueD6$ equal pieces. So the $\blueD{\text{denominator}}$ is $\blueD6$.

$\purpleD{\dfrac{?}\blueD{6}}$

There are $\purpleD{10}$ pieces shaded. So, the $\purpleD{\text{numerator}}$ is $\purpleD{10}$.

$\purpleD{\dfrac{10}\blueD{6}}$
The shaded amount is equal to $\purpleD{\dfrac{10}\blueD{6}}$.

khan_exercises.description: Identify the fraction of a whole that is shaded.  Fractions are greater than or equal to one.

items.question_content:
Each circle represents $1$ whole.



[[☃ image 1]]

**What fraction does the total area shaded represent?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
The $\purpleD{\text{numerator}}$ is the number of equal parts being counted.

The $\blueD{\text{denominator}}$ is the number of equal parts in **one** whole. 

$\dfrac{\purpleD{\text{numerator}}}{\blueD{\text{denominator}}}$

Each whole is divided into $\blueD6$ equal pieces. So the $\blueD{\text{denominator}}$ is $\blueD6$.

$\purpleD{\dfrac{?}\blueD{6}}$

There are $\purpleD{10}$ pieces shaded. So, the $\purpleD{\text{numerator}}$ is $\purpleD{10}$.

The shaded amount is equal to $\purpleD{\dfrac{10}\blueD{6}}$.

khan_exercises.title: Identify unit fractions
khan_exercises.description: Identify unit fractions when given a visual or a context.
topic_path: Module 5: Fractions as numbers on the number line
Topic A: Partitioning a whole into equal parts

items.question_content:
The sun hits $1$ of the $4$ equal-sized sections of an ice skating rink more than the others.  This section is extra slippery because the sun has melted it some.

[[☃ image 1]]

 
**What fraction of the ice skating rink is extra slippery because of the sun?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints: There are $4$ sections, and they are all the same size. The total number of equal parts is $4$.
When we have $4$ equal parts, then the area of each part is $\dfrac{1}{4}$ of the total area.
Since the sun hits $1$ section more than the others, we say that  $\dfrac{1}{4}$ , or one-fourth, of the ice skating rink is extra slippery.

options.alt: A circle divided into four equal parts with one part shaded.

items.question_content:
Scarlett cuts the grass in her grandparents' yard.  Her grandparents' yard is made up of $2$ equal-sized sections.  So far, Scarlett has cut the grass in $1$ section.     

[[☃ image 1]]

 
**What fraction of the grass has Scarlett cut?**   

[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints: There are $2$ sections, and they are all the same size. The total number of equal parts is $2$.
When we have $2$ equal parts, then the area of each part is $\dfrac{1}{2}$ of the total area.
Since Scarlett has only cut $1$ section, we say that she has cut $\dfrac{1}{2}$ , or one-half, of the grass.

options.alt: A square divided into two equal parts with one part shaded.

khan_exercises.title: Recognize fractions
khan_exercises.description: Identify the fraction of a whole that is shaded.  Fractions are less than 1.

items.question_content:
The following circle is equal to one whole.


[[☃ image 1]]

**What fraction of the circle is shaded?**



[[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
There are $\maroonD6$ equal parts.


[[☃ image 1]]

Each slice is equal to $\blueD1$ out of $\maroonD6$ pieces, or ${\dfrac{\blueD1}\maroonD{6}}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

The shaded amount is equal to ${\dfrac{\blueD2}\maroonD{6}} $.There are $\blueD{2}$ out of $\maroonD6$ slices shaded, or ${\dfrac{\blueD2}\maroonD{6}}$.


[[☃ image 1]]

options.alt: A circle divided into six equal parts with two parts shaded.

khan_exercises.title: Fractions of different wholes
khan_exercises.description: Compare two fractions based on the whole that each one refers to.
topic_path: Module 5: Fractions as numbers on the number line
Topic C: Comparing unit fractions and specifying the whole

items.question_content:
Jose went to a restaurant and ate $\dfrac13$ of the medium pizza.  

His sister Nina stayed home and ate $\dfrac13$ of a smaller pizza that their mother baked.

**Who ate more pizza?**  


[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We don't know the size of $\red{\text{Nina's}}$ pizza, but we know it was *smaller* than $\green{\text{Jose's}}$ pizza.


$\qquad$

[[☃ image 1]]

$\qquad$

[[☃ image 2]]
Since $\red{\text{Nina's}}$ pizza was *smaller* than  $\green{\text{Jose's}}$ pizza, the $\dfrac13$ that $\red{\text{Nina}}$ ate is *less* than the $\dfrac13$ that $\green{\text{Jose's}}$ ate.
Jose ate more pizza.

choices.content: Jose
Nina
They ate the same amount.

items.question_content:
While the bears were out, Goldilocks ate all but $\dfrac38$ of Papa Bear's big green cake off his plate.

Then she ate all but $\dfrac38$ of the smaller pink cake on Baby Bear's plate.

**Whose plate had more cake left on it?**  


 
[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
$\pink{\text{Baby Bear's}}$ cake was *smaller* than $\green{\text{Papa Bear's}}$ cake.

$\qquad$

[[☃ image 1]]

$\qquad$

[[☃ image 2]]



The amount of cake left on $\dfrac38$ of $\green{\text{Papa Bear's}}$ plate is *more* than the amount of cake left on $\dfrac38$ of $\pink{\text{Baby Bear's}}$ plate.
Papa Bear's plate had more cake left on it.

choices.content: Papa Bear's plate
Baby Bear's plate
Both plates had the same amount of cake left.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]
The horse is taller.

choices.content: The cow
The horse
They both were the same height.

items.question_content:
In the video game Fantasy Farm, it takes $\dfrac56$ of a *minute* to harvest magic flowers, and it takes $\dfrac56$ of an *hour* to build a golden barn.

**Which takes more time?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: $\green{\text{Harvesting magic flowers}}$ takes $\dfrac56$ of a $\green{\text{minute}}$.

$\blue{\text{Building a golden barn}}$ takes $\dfrac56$ of an $\blue{\text{hour}}$.

We know that an $\blue{\text{hour}}$ is more than a $\green{\text{minute}}$, so $\dfrac56$ of an $\blue{\text{hour}}$ is more than $\dfrac56$ of a $\green{\text{minute}}$.
Building a golden barn takes more time.

choices.content: Harvesting magic flowers
Building a golden barn
They take the same amount of time.

$\qquad$

[[☃ image 1]]


[[☃ image 2]]

There is *more* milkshake in $\dfrac38$ of a *larger* glass than in $\dfrac38$ of a smaller glass.
Rita had more milkshake left to drink.

[[☃ radio 1]]

$\red{\text{Caleb}}$ painted a wall that was the same size as $\blue{\text{Eli's}}$ wall.

$\qquad$

$\qquad$

[[☃ image 2]]
The $\dfrac34$ of the wall that $\blue{\text{Eli}}$ painted has the same area as the $\dfrac34$ of the wall that $\red{\text{Caleb}}$ painted.
Eli and Caleb painted the same area.

choices.content: Eli
Caleb
They painted the same amount.

items.question_content:
It took Hiro $\dfrac13$ of an hour to run from his house to school.

It took Fred $\dfrac13$ of an hour to run from his house to school.

**Who took more time running to school?**  

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
We know that Hiro ran for $\dfrac13$ of an hour.  


We know that Fred ran for $\dfrac13$ of an hour.
Since an hour is always the same amount of time, $\dfrac13$ of an hour is always the same amount of time, too.
Both activities took the same amount of time.

choices.content: Hiro
Fred
Both took the same amount of time.
